Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - Weapons and Firearms
The weapons framework spans firearms (regulated under Firearms Acts 1925-2006), offensive weapons (s.9
NFOAP 1997), and knife offences (s.9A NFOAP and related). This chapter sets out the operational framework.

1.1 The Firearms Acts Framework
The principal firearms statutes:
Statute

Principal Content

Firearms Act 1925

Foundational; possession licensing

Firearms Act 1964

Carrying firearms with intent

Firearms Act 1971

Restricted firearms; further offences

Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act
1990

Modernisation; offensive weapons; imitation firearms

Firearms (Firearm Certificates for NonResidents) Act 2000

Non-resident certificate framework

Criminal Justice Act 2006 / 2007

Enhanced firearms offences; mandatory minimum sentences

Criminal
Justice
Provisions) Acts

Periodic updates

(Miscellaneous

1.2 The Firearm Certificate Framework
Firearms generally require a Firearm Certificate issued by Superintendent.
Specified firearms - restricted firearms - higher threshold; Chief Superintendent.
Categories: shotguns (most common - sporting use); rifles (sporting / professional); restricted firearms (rare very specific purposes).
Renewal periodic; conditions on certificate.
Suitability assessment - safe storage, good reason, fitness.
Surrender and revocation procedures.

1.3 Principal Firearms Offences
1.3.1 Possession Without Certificate - s.2 Firearms Act 1925
A person shall not possess, use, or carry any firearm or ammunition unless he holds a firearm certificate.
Possession without certificate is an offence. Penalty: up to 5 years on indictment (general); higher for restricted
firearms.

1.3.2 Possession of Firearm with Intent to Endanger Life - s.15 Firearms Act 1925
Possession of any firearm or ammunition with intent to endanger life. Up to life imprisonment on indictment.
Mandatory minimum 5 years (subject to exceptional circumstances exception similar to s.15A MDA).

1.3.3 Possession of Firearm with Intent to Commit Indictable Offence
Possession with intent to commit an indictable offence. Substantial penalties.

1.3.4 Discharging Firearm with Intent to Resist Arrest
Discharging a firearm with intent to resist arrest. Substantial penalties.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.3.5 Possession of Restricted Firearm
Restricted firearms (machine guns; certain pistols; specified high-power weapons) - even with certificate,
additional offences and mandatory minimum sentences. Up to life imprisonment.

1.3.6 Possession in Public Place - s.27A Firearms Act 1964
Possession of firearm in a public place. Aggravated form of possession. Substantial penalties.

1.4 Offensive Weapons - s.9 NFOAP 1997
s.9 NFOAP 1997

Elements
In a public place: RTA-equivalent definition - public places, places of public resort.
Any knife / bladed / sharply pointed article: Wide scope - includes folding knives, kitchen knives,
screwdrivers used as weapons.
OR offensive weapon: Any article made / adapted / intended for causing injury.
Without good reason or lawful authority: Defence - work tool, ceremonial, sporting use.

Operational Application
Most commonly applied: knife in public place.
'Adapted metal pole' and similar improvised weapons - offensive weapon limb.
Stop and search powers under s.30 NFOAP (offensive weapon-specific) or s.23 MDA (combined search).
Penalty: up to 5 years on indictment.

1.5 Imitation / Replica Firearms
Imitation firearms (replicas, BB guns, certain blank-firing weapons) can be the subject of:
Specific offences (possession in public place; possession with intent).
Use to intimidate / threaten - offensive weapon plus relevant substantive offence (e.g., aggravated robbery).
Manufacturing offences.

1.6 Stop, Search, Seize Powers
s.30 NFOAP 1997 - search for offensive weapons on reasonable suspicion.
s.23 MDA - combined search where drugs / weapons suspected together.
Common-law search incidental to arrest.
Search warrant for premises where firearms suspected (Damache framework).
Seizure - exhibit handling chain critical given trial significance.

1.7 Firearms Investigation
Ballistics examination - Garda Ballistics Section.
Forensic links - DNA, fingerprints, GSR (gunshot residue).
Chain of custody - critical given evidential stakes.
Specialist Detective engagement in serious cases - National Bureau of Criminal Investigation (NBCI), Armed
Support Unit (ASU).
Communications data and surveillance often supplements.

1.8 Armed Operations - The Garda Framework

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Most Gardai are unarmed. Armed support is provided through specific units.
Armed Support Unit (ASU) - regional armed Garda response.
Emergency Response Unit (ERU) - national specialist armed unit; hostage / barricade / armed suspect
operations.
Authorised Firearms Officers (AFOs) - trained Gardai authorised to carry firearms in specific roles.
Threshold for use of firearm - strict; specific framework; accountability after.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Communications Data Framework
Communications data - retained telecommunications data accessed by Gardai for investigation - has been
substantially reshaped by CJEU jurisprudence and Irish statutory reform. This chapter sets out the operational
framework as it currently operates.

2.1 The Statutory Architecture
Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 - foundational Irish framework.
Communications (Retention of Data) (Amendment) Act 2022 - reform following CJEU jurisprudence.
CJEU framework: Digital Rights Ireland (2014); Tele2 Sverige / Watson (2016); La Quadrature du Net (2020);
Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Dwyer (2022 - Irish referral).
ECHR Article 8 - privacy considerations.

2.2 The Categories of Communications Data
Category

Examples

Subscriber data

Name, address associated with phone number / IP address

Traffic data

Call records, message timing, contact patterns

Location data

Cell site information, IP location

Content data

Actual contents of messages / calls - requires separate framework, typically
interception order

Different categories engage different statutory authorities and access thresholds. Content interception is a
separate framework not commonly available to standard investigation.

2.3 The Access Framework (Post-2022)
Access to retained communications data requires statutory authority.
Authority typically from court / independent body for serious crime investigation.
Specific frameworks for emergency access (e.g., missing person life-at-risk situations).
Disclosure to investigation by service provider follows authority production.
Authorisation documentation becomes part of investigation file.

2.4 Operational Use
Missing persons - last cell site activity; phone location; contact analysis.
Murder / serious crime - suspect movement; contact analysis; pattern of life.
Drug trafficking - communications between suspects; supply chain mapping.
Organised crime - associate networks; activity coordination.
Sexual offences - grooming evidence; contact between suspect and complainant.

2.5 CJEU Jurisprudence - Operational Implications
2.5.1 Digital Rights Ireland (2014)
CJEU invalidated the EU Data Retention Directive on Charter privacy grounds. Indiscriminate retention
disproportionate. Triggered reform of national frameworks.

2.5.2 Tele2 Sverige / Watson (2016)
CJEU held that general and indiscriminate retention of data was incompatible with EU law. Retention must be

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

targeted and proportionate; access subject to prior independent review.

2.5.3 La Quadrature du Net (2020)
CJEU clarified the framework - targeted retention permitted for serious crime / national security; expedited
preservation; specific safeguards.

2.5.4 Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Dwyer (2022)
Irish-CJEU referral on the Irish framework. Operationally important - drove the 2022 amendments to the Irish
statute.

2.6 The 2022 Irish Framework
Targeted retention - replacing general retention with specific categories / purposes.
Independent authorisation for access - typically court.
Expedited preservation framework for live cases.
Specific safeguards for journalistic / privileged sources.
Oversight by designated judge / authority.
Operational adjustment - the framework continues to develop; verify current provisions.

2.7 Practical Investigation Steps

2.8 Privileged Material
Journalistic source material - additional safeguards.
Lawyer-client communications - privilege framework.
Medical / counselling communications - sensitive disclosure.
Where privileged material identified in data set, segregation and judicial review of access.

2.9 Common Operational Issues
Authority threshold not met for the type / period of data sought.
Request over-broad - data sought beyond investigation need.
Service provider compliance delays.
Format / interpretation issues - GCCB or specialist analysis needed.
Cross-jurisdictional - data held by foreign service provider; MLAT framework.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - PULSE and PEMS
PULSE (Police Using Leading Systems Effectively) and PEMS (Property and Exhibit Management System)
are the Garda information systems that underpin investigation file management. This chapter sets out their
operational role.

3.1 PULSE - Incident and Investigation Management
PULSE is the principal Garda IT system for incident recording, investigation management, and intelligence.
Every incident has a PULSE record (PID - PULSE Incident ID).
Incident classification - by offence type and circumstances.
Investigation log - steps recorded over time.
Person records - linked across incidents.
Vehicle records.
Intelligence records (separate framework).
Reports generated for management, analysis, public information.

3.1.1 PULSE Entry Standards
Accurate - reflects actual facts as known.
Contemporaneous - entries at time of incident / step.
Comprehensive - relevant information captured.
Confidential - access controls; need-to-know.
Updated - status / classification / outcomes maintained.

3.1.2 PULSE Integrity Issues
Inaccurate entries - operational and disciplinary concern.
Backdating - serious integrity issue.
Failure to update - case status drift.
Improper access - need-to-know principle.
PULSE entries are evidentially significant - admissible at trial; disclosable to defence.

3.2 PEMS - Property and Exhibit Management
PEMS is the Garda exhibit management system.
Every exhibit logged - unique reference.
Chain of custody tracked - who received, when, transferred to where.
Storage location.
Disposal / return tracking after case conclusion.
Underpins chain of custody evidence at trial.

3.2.1 PEMS Operational Standards
Immediate logging on seizure.
Unique exhibit number assigned.
Description recorded.
Tamper-evident sealing where appropriate.
Each transfer signed and recorded.
Storage location secure.
Periodic audit.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.3 Other Garda Information Systems
Garda Information Sharing System (GISS) - intelligence sharing.
Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) - vehicle tracking.
Body Worn Video (BWV) system - video evidence management.
Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) - control room operational system.
Various specialist databases - drug intelligence, criminal records, etc.

3.4 The PEMS / FSI / MBRS Interface
Drug investigation specifics: exhibits logged in PEMS at seizure; transported to FSI / MBRS per chain of
custody; results returned; exhibits stored pending court / disposal.

3.5 PULSE as Disclosure Source
PULSE incident details - disclosable to defence as relevant.
Prior incidents involving suspect / complainant - disclosable subject to relevance / privilege.
Investigation log - disclosable as record of investigation.
Sensitivity considerations - sources, intelligence material, third-party privacy - require careful disclosure
analysis.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - Command and Control
Garda command and control framework operates across routine policing and major incidents. The structure
provides clear lines of decision-making, accountability, and operational coordination.

4.1 The Standard Hierarchy
Rank

Role

Garda

Operational - frontline policing

Sergeant

Supervisory - shift, area, function

Inspector

Functional / district leadership

Superintendent

District / divisional command

Chief Superintendent

Divisional command / specialist functions

Assistant Commissioner

Regional / functional command

Deputy
Commissioner
Commissioner

/

National command

4.2 Routine Operational Command
Shift Sergeant - tactical oversight of shift.
Member in Charge - custody operational decisions.
Detective Sergeant - investigative leadership at District.
Inspector - extended-shift / operations of scale.
Superintendent - District-wide policy and major decisions.

4.3 Major Incident Command - Gold / Silver / Bronze
4.3.1 Gold (Strategic)
Strategic command - typically Assistant Commissioner / Chief Superintendent for very major incidents.
Sets strategic objectives.
Resource authorisation at high level.
Multi-agency liaison at strategic level.
Off-scene typically; in command centre.

4.3.2 Silver (Tactical)
Tactical command - typically Superintendent or Chief Superintendent.
Translates Gold objectives into tactical plan.
Resource deployment decisions.
Forward command typically at or near scene.

4.3.3 Bronze (Operational)
Operational command - typically Inspector / Sergeant.
Implements tactical plan.
Direct on-scene command of operational resources.
Multiple Bronze commanders possible for different aspects.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.4 Specific Operational Command Frameworks
Critical Incident Commander - for hostage / barricade / armed incidents.
Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) - major criminal investigation lead.
Public Order Commander - sustained public order operations.
Search Commander - missing persons / scene search operations.
Fatal Collision Investigation - FCI scene command.

4.5 The Decision Log
Major incidents require contemporaneous decision logging.
Decision Log records: what decision; by whom; rationale; alternatives considered; time.
Essential for after-action review, judicial review, inquest, criminal proceedings.
Honesty in entry; do not write retrospectively.

4.6 The Garda Decision-Making Model
Standard structured decision-making framework used by Gardai operationally:
The framework supports lawful, proportionate, defensible decision-making under operational pressure.

4.7 Multi-Agency Coordination
Major Emergency Management (MEM) framework - Gardai, Fire, NAS / HSE, Local Authority.
National Emergency Coordination Centre activation in major emergencies.
Joint command arrangements - aligned with each agency's command structure.
Common terminology and command protocols.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 5 - Specialist Resources Consolidated
This chapter consolidates the Garda specialist resources referenced across Volumes 1-8 into a single operational
reference.

5.1 Specialist Investigation Bureaux
Bureau
National
Bureau
Investigation (NBCI)

Function
of

Criminal

Serious crime - murder, kidnap, organised crime, robbery

Garda National Drugs and Organised
Crime Bureau (DOCB)

Drug trafficking, organised crime, major operations

Garda National Protective Services
Bureau (DSCB)

Sexual crime, child protection, missing persons, online child
exploitation

Garda National Economic Crime Bureau
(GNECB)

Fraud, money laundering, financial crime

Garda National Cyber Crime Bureau
(GCCB)

Cyber crime, digital forensic, online offences

Garda National Immigration Bureau
(GNIB)

Immigration / human trafficking - victim and enforcement

Criminal Assets Bureau (CAB)

Multi-agency civil proceeds-of-crime

Garda National Anti-Corruption Bureau
(GNACB)

Corruption / Garda discipline serious investigations

5.2 Operational / Response Units
Unit

Function

Emergency Response Unit (ERU)

National specialist tactical - hostage, barricade, armed suspect

Armed Support Unit (ASU)

Regional armed response

Public Order Unit (POU)

Public order at scale; riot response

Air Support Unit

Aerial reconnaissance, search

Water Unit / Dive Team

Water-based search; underwater recovery

Mounted Unit

Crowd / event policing; community

Garda Dog Unit

Search, detection, public order

5.3 Forensic and Technical Resources
Resource

Function

Garda Technical Bureau

Photography, mapping, fingerprint, ballistics

Forensic Science Ireland (FSI)

Biological, DNA, drug analysis, toxicology, trace

Medical Bureau of Road Safety (MBRS)

Alcohol / drug analysis for evidential RTA samples

Crime Scene Investigators (CSI)

Divisional / Regional - scene examination

State Pathologist / Deputy

Forensic post-mortem in suspicious deaths

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Resource

Function

Forensic Collision Investigator (FCI)

Fatal collision scene reconstruction

5.4 Liaison / Specialist Roles at District / Divisional Level
Domestic Abuse Liaison Officer / DARI (Domestic Abuse / Sexual Crime Investigation Unit).
Children First Officer.
Family Liaison Officer (FLO).
Sex Offender Management Unit liaison.
Specialist Victim Interviewer.
Diversity Officer.
Community Engagement Officer.
Roads Policing Unit member.

5.5 External Specialist Partners
Tusla - Child and Family Agency.
HSE Mental Health Services.
Sexual Assault Treatment Units (SATU).
Coroners.
State Pathologist.
Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission (GSOC).
Customs / Revenue.
Immigration.
Prisons Service.
Probation Service.
Specialist NGOs - Women's Aid, Dublin RCC, CARI, Pieta House, Samaritans, etc.

5.6 Engaging Specialist Resources
Threshold for engagement - calibrated to incident severity and operational need.
Engagement typically via Sergeant / Inspector / supervisor.
Documented in PULSE / decision log.
Coordination with arriving specialists - handover briefing, command arrangements clarified.
Continuing local Garda role - typically support and scene-management even after specialists in lead.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 6 - Custody Framework Consolidated
The Garda custody framework spans multiple statutes with different detention regimes for different offence
categories. This chapter consolidates the regimes into a single operational reference.

6.1 The Detention Regimes - Overview
Statute

Application

Maximum Duration

Criminal Justice Act 1984 s.4

General arrestable offences

24 hours in stages

Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act
1996 s.2

Drug trafficking offences

7 days in stages

Offences Against the State Act 1939 s.30
(and amendments)

Specified offences (terrorism / organised
crime)

Up to 72 hours; extension
by District Court further

Criminal Justice Act 2007 s.50

Specified serious offences

Extended
framework

s.42 CJA 1999

Persons in lawful custody for other
offence

Specific framework

6.2 The CJA 1984 Standard Regime
6.2.1 Initial Detention
Arrest under lawful power (Walsh reasonable suspicion).
Transport to Garda station - typically the nearest.
Custody process by Member in Charge.
Personal details; reason for arrest; notification rights explained.
Booking in - personal effects accounted; clothing condition; visible injuries documented.
Initial detention period - 6 hours from arrival.

6.2.2 Extension Framework
Stage

Authority

Maximum

Initial

Member in Charge

6 hours from arrival

First extension

Superintendent (s.4(3))

+6 hours (12 total)

Second extension

Chief Superintendent (s.4(4))

+12 hours (24 total)

6.2.3 Authorisation Standard
Each extension requires authority of officer of required rank.
Authority must be satisfied of necessity for further detention.
Standard: reasonable grounds to believe further detention necessary for proper investigation.
Suspect informed of extension.
Documented in custody record.

6.3 Drug Trafficking Act 1996 7-Day Regime

6.4 The Suspect's Rights in Custody
Right to know the reason for arrest.
Right to consult a solicitor (Gormley framework).
Right to solicitor present in interview (Doyle framework).
Right to notify family / nominated person (subject to investigation considerations).
Right to medical attention where required.
Right to food and rest periods.
Right to interpreter where required.
Right to access embassy / consular (foreign nationals).
Right to be informed of detention extensions.

6.5 The Member-in-Charge Function
Member in Charge (typically Sergeant) - responsible for custody operational integrity.
Independent of investigation - operates as welfare guardian.
Custody Regulations 1987 framework.
Decisions on: booking in, medical needs, rest periods, telephone calls, charge decisions, release / continued
detention.
Maintains custody record.

6.6 The Custody Record
Contemporaneous record of every custody event.
Times of arrival, processing steps, rights communicated, interviews, meals, rest periods, medical attendance,
solicitor contact.
Foundational evidence in any subsequent proceedings.
Reviewed in any disputed-custody case.
Preservation per evidence retention framework.

6.7 Interview Within Custody
Pre-interview consultation (Gormley) facilitated.
Caution administered.
Interview recording (electronic - audio and increasingly video).
Solicitor presence where requested (Doyle).
Suitable for interview (fit, not under influence) - Madden voluntariness.
Statutory inferences framework (s.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984) - Buck compliance.
Reasonable duration; breaks; sustenance.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

6.8 Special Categories
6.8.1 Juveniles - Children Act 2001
Specific juvenile framework - distinct from adult custody.
Garda Juvenile Liaison Officer (JLO) engagement.
Parent / guardian / appropriate adult notification.
Specific facilities where available.
Restorative justice / cautioning consideration.

6.8.2 Vulnerable Adults
Mental health, learning disability, intoxication.
Appropriate adult engagement.
Medical assessment.
Specialist interviewer where appropriate.

6.8.3 Mental Health in Custody
Cross-reference Vol 8 Chapter 3.
s.12 MHA 2001 redirection where criteria met.
Suicide / self-harm prevention.
Approved centre admission where indicated.

6.9 Release / Charge Decision
DPP direction on charging in many cases.
Member in Charge can release without charge in straightforward cases.
Charge framework - charge sheet completion; bail considerations.
Conditional release (bail) framework operates.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 7 - Disclosure Framework
Disclosure - the prosecution's obligation to disclose to the defence material relevant to the case - is a
foundational fair-trial principle. This chapter sets out the operational framework.

7.1 The Disclosure Principle
Article 38.1 Constitution / Article 6 ECHR - fair trial right.
Prosecution must disclose material that may assist defence or undermine prosecution.
Continuous obligation - through trial and any appeal.
Failure to disclose material can result in case dismissal, conviction overturned, professional discipline.

7.2 The Categories of Material
7.2.1 Standard Investigation Material
Statements of witnesses called / not called.
Notebook entries of involved Gardai.
BWV footage.
CCTV obtained.
Forensic reports.
Custody records.
Interview transcripts.
Charge sheets and bail material.

7.2.2 Material That May Assist Defence
Inconsistencies in witness accounts.
Witnesses who did not support prosecution narrative.
Material undermining prosecution case.
Material on complainant credibility (limited - subject to specific frameworks).
Forensic findings that complicate prosecution case.

7.2.3 Sensitive Material
Intelligence sources / methods.
Privileged communications (lawyer-client, doctor-patient, journalist-source).
Third-party private information.
Material from ongoing investigation.
National security material.

7.3 The Public Interest Immunity (PII) Process
Where sensitive material would damage public interest if disclosed, PII application.
Court reviews material; weighs public interest against fair trial.
Court can order: full disclosure; redacted disclosure; non-disclosure with appropriate safeguards.
DPP / counsel manages PII process.

7.4 Specific Sensitive Categories
7.4.1 Complainant Counselling Records (Sexual Offences)
Vol 6 framework - privilege; defence application; court balancing; redaction common.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

7.4.2 Children First / Tusla Material
Statutory framework for sharing within investigation.
Children's welfare considerations in disclosure.

7.4.3 Medical / Mental Health Records
Privacy framework; consent typically required.
Court order may compel.

7.4.4 Intelligence Material
Garda intelligence framework operates separately.
Material developed from intelligence may need PII to protect source.

7.5 The Disclosure File
Investigation file is foundation.
Disclosure register - itemised list of all material; status of disclosure.
Sensitive material flagged.
Continuing obligation - update as material emerges.

7.6 Operational Standards
Investigation file built with disclosure in mind from outset.
Every step documented - the basis of disclosure is the investigation record.
Sensitive material flagged early - PII analysis prepared by DPP.
Liaison with DPP / counsel on disclosure issues.
Compliance with directions on additional disclosure.

7.7 Common Disclosure Issues
Material discovered late in trial preparation.
Material from co-investigators not previously surfaced.
Sensitive material requiring PII process.
Volume of disclosure in major investigations - file management critical.
Failure to disclose - severe consequences.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Statutory Anchors (Vol 9 - Cross-Cutting)
Part A - Firearms Acts Architecture
Firearms Act 1925 - foundational; s.2 possession without certificate; s.15 with intent to endanger life.
Firearms Act 1964 - s.27A possession in public place.
Firearms Act 1971 - restricted firearms.
Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990 - modernisation; offensive weapons; imitation firearms.
Firearms (Firearm Certificates for Non-Residents) Act 2000.
Criminal Justice Act 2006 / 2007 - enhanced firearms offences; mandatory minimum sentences.

Part B - Offensive Weapons - NFOAP 1997
s.9 - possession of knife / sharply pointed article / offensive weapon in public place.
s.9A - aggravated form.
s.10 - production of weapon during commission of offence.
s.30 - search power.

Part C - Communications Data
Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 - foundational Irish framework.
Communications (Retention of Data) (Amendment) Act 2022 - post-CJEU reform.
Various Statutory Instruments giving effect to the framework.
Postal and Telecommunications Services Act 1983 (lawful interception framework - separate from retained
data).

Part D - Detention Framework
Criminal Justice Act 1984 - s.4 standard detention; ss.18, 19, 19A inferences.
Custody Regulations 1987.
Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 - s.2 extended detention.
Offences Against the State Act 1939 - s.30 specified-offence detention.
Criminal Justice Act 2007 - s.50 further extended detention for serious offences.
Criminal Justice (Amendment) Act 2009 - further provisions.

Part E - PULSE / PEMS Framework
Garda Síochána Act 2005 - statutory framework for Garda functions and accountability.
Garda HQ directives and standard operating procedures (internal framework).
Data Protection Acts - GDPR-compliant data processing.

Part F - Command Framework
Garda Síochána Act 2005 - hierarchy and powers framework.
Major Emergency Management framework - Government policy / multi-agency.
Garda Public Order Strategy.
Garda Pursuit Policy.

Part G - Disclosure Framework
Article 38.1 Constitution - fair trial.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Article 6 ECHR - fair trial.
Common-law disclosure obligations developed through case law.
DPP Guidelines on Disclosure - operational framework.
PII framework - Constitutional and common-law.

Part H - Garda Síochána Act 2005 - Foundational
Garda Commissioner; structure of force.
Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission (GSOC).
Garda Síochána Inspectorate.
Policing Authority (now superseded by Policing, Security and Community Safety arrangements).
Garda Code of Ethics framework.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 9 - Cross-Cutting
Reference)
Volume 9 consolidates the cross-cutting case law that runs through Garda investigation: the CJEU
communications-data jurisprudence (Digital Rights Ireland through Dwyer); the firearms / weapons authorities;
the detention framework authorities (across the multiple statutes); and the disclosure framework (Constitutional
fair trial / Article 6 ECHR / Marks-style).

Part A - CJEU Communications Data Jurisprudence
A.1 Digital Rights Ireland (Joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12, 2014)
Citation: Joined Cases C-293/12 and C-594/12, Court of Justice of the European Union, judgment 8 April
2014.
Facts: Digital Rights Ireland (an NGO) and Austrian applicants challenged the EU Data Retention Directive
(2006/24/EC) on Charter privacy grounds (Articles 7 and 8 EU Charter). The Directive required Member States
to ensure providers retained communications data for periods of 6 months to 2 years for law enforcement
purposes.
Holding: Directive invalid. Indiscriminate retention across the entire population, without targeting or
proportionate safeguards, was incompatible with the Charter privacy rights.
Reasoning: CJEU held that the Directive's blanket approach was disproportionate. Retention should be
targeted; access should be subject to prior independent review; data should be retained within the EU; specific
safeguards required.
Significance: Foundational. Invalidated the EU-wide retention framework. Required all Member States to
reform their national frameworks. Started the line of jurisprudence that has reshaped communications-data law.
Operational Application: Irish framework reformed in response. The 2022 Act framework operates within
the post-Digital Rights principles. Garda access to communications data operates within the targeted,
proportionate, judicially-supervised framework.

A.2 Tele2 Sverige / Watson (Joined Cases C-203/15 and C-698/15, 2016)
Citation: Joined Cases C-203/15 (Tele2 Sverige) and C-698/15 (Watson), CJEU judgment 21 December 2016.
Facts: Swedish and UK national retention frameworks (post-Digital Rights Ireland) maintained general
retention with provider obligation to retain. National courts referred questions on EU law compliance.
Holding: General and indiscriminate retention - even where ostensibly for serious crime - was incompatible
with the Charter. Access by national authorities must be subject to prior review by a court or independent body;
data should be retained within the EU; effective remedy must be available.
Reasoning: CJEU reinforced and extended Digital Rights principles. The fact of national security / serious
crime objective did not justify blanket retention. Targeted retention (specific persons / locations / time periods)
on basis of objective evidence was the framework.
Significance: Required further national reform. The targeted-retention framework became the operational
standard. Prior independent authorisation for access became required.
Operational Application: Irish framework adjusted. Authorisation regimes for access reformed. The 2022 Act
framework reflects Tele2 / Watson requirements. Practical operational impact - access to retained data requires
the proper authorisation framework; broad / speculative requests no longer compatible.

A.3 La Quadrature du Net and Others (Joined Cases C-511/18 etc., 2020)
Citation: Joined Cases C-511/18, C-512/18 and C-520/18, CJEU Grand Chamber judgment 6 October 2020.
Facts: French and Belgian national retention frameworks challenged. Further clarification sought on the postTele2 framework.
Holding: CJEU clarified the post-Tele2 framework. General retention impermissible for general crime;
targeted retention permitted; specific categories (IP addresses, civil identity) treated differently; expedited

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

preservation framework recognised; national security gives somewhat more latitude but within strict limits.
Reasoning: The CJEU recognised that operational realities require some flexibility - real-time threats, urgent
investigations, identifiable specific categories. The framework permits structured targeted retention with
appropriate safeguards. Different categories of data have different privacy weight and may justify different
retention.
Significance: Operational framework refined. Recognised expedited preservation (where specific events justify
rapid retention of data that would otherwise be deleted). Specific categories framework provided more workable
operational base.
Operational Application: Irish framework operates within La Quadrature principles. Expedited preservation
framework for live cases; targeted retention; specific category treatment. The 2022 Act and subsequent
operational policy reflect these refinements.

A.4 Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Dwyer (Case C-140/20, CJEU 2022)
Citation: Case C-140/20, CJEU judgment 5 April 2022.
Facts: Dwyer had been convicted of murder of Elaine O'Hara. Communications data evidence was central to
the prosecution. He challenged the Irish Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 on EU law grounds.
The Supreme Court referred to CJEU.
Holding: Irish 2011 framework not compliant with EU law in respect of general and indiscriminate retention.
However, evidence obtained under the framework, prior to the framework being held incompatible, was not
automatically inadmissible - national procedural autonomy applied subject to equivalence and effectiveness.
Reasoning: CJEU reaffirmed Tele2 / La Quadrature principles for the Irish context. The Member State
framework must be reformed. Past evidence required analysis under Irish law - the conviction itself was not
automatically tainted but the framework going forward had to change.
Significance: Specifically Irish significance - drove the Communications (Retention of Data) (Amendment)
Act 2022 reform. Operational consequence: the Irish framework has been substantially rewritten to comply.
Operational Application: Garda operational practice for communications data has been adjusted. Current
operational framework operates within the post-Dwyer / 2022 Act standards. Pre-2022 evidence cases analysed
on case-specific basis. The framework continues to develop.

A.5 Privacy International (Case C-623/17, CJEU 2020)
Significance: Companion to La Quadrature - national security framework engagement. Recognises some
additional latitude for national security but within strict limits and judicial supervision.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part B - Firearms and Weapons Authorities
B.1 The Firearms Mandatory Minimum Sentence Framework
Firearms Act 1925 s.15 (possession with intent to endanger life) and related provisions carry mandatory
minimum sentences subject to exceptional and specific circumstances exception (similar to s.15A MDA). Case
law has developed on the exception framework similarly to drugs:
Plea timing.
Cooperation.
Role analysis.
Personal circumstances.
Nature of firearm.
Context of possession.

B.2 The People (DPP) v O'Driscoll - Firearms Sentencing
Court of Criminal Appeal authority on firearms sentencing. The structured discretion framework (Tiernan)
applies. Aggravators: type of firearm; loaded / unloaded; intent established; conviction record; gang context;
use in offence.

B.3 Procedural Authorities
Standard Volume 2 procedural framework applies to firearms cases - Walsh, Madden, Buck, Gormley, Doyle,
JC, Damache, Casey. Specific procedural rigour given the case stakes.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part C - Detention Framework Authorities
C.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - The Arrest Standard
Walsh remains foundational across all detention regimes. Each arrest requires the arresting Garda's articulable
reasonable suspicion.

C.2 Detention Regulations Compliance
The Custody Regulations 1987 framework underpins the CJA 1984 regime. Compliance with the Regulations
is part of the lawfulness of detention. Specific authorities on:
Member-in-Charge function independence.
Rights notification.
Welfare framework.
Custody record integrity.

C.3 Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17
Pre-interview solicitor consultation - applied across all detention regimes.

C.4 Doyle [2017] IESC 1
Solicitor presence in interview where requested - applied across regimes.

C.5 Buck [2002] 2 IR 268
Inferences framework - strict prerequisites across detention regimes.

C.6 Constitutional Detention Authority
Article 40.4 Constitution - the inquiry into the legality of detention (habeas corpus equivalent). The framework
supervises all detention; unlawful detention is judicially correctable.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part D - Disclosure Framework Authorities
D.1 Article 38.1 / Article 6 ECHR - Fair Trial
Disclosure is part of the fair trial right. The investigation and prosecution must provide the defence with material
relevant to its case.

D.2 Irish Authorities on Disclosure
DPP guidance on disclosure - operational framework.
Specific authorities on scope, sensitive material, PII process.
Continuing obligation through trial and appeal.

D.3 The PII Framework
Public Interest Immunity - balancing fair trial against legitimate public interest in withholding.
Court-conducted balancing.
Specific procedures for review of sensitive material.
Operational engagement of DPP / counsel.

D.4 The Marks Framework (Inferences from Non-Disclosure)
Where prosecution fails to disclose material that emerges later, court may consider the impact on fair trial exclusion of evidence; case dismissal; conviction overturned.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part E - Key Operational Takeaways
E.1 The Communications Data Compliance Standard
Access only under proper statutory framework with appropriate authority. Targeted requests; specificity to
investigation need; documented authorisation; integration with chain of custody.

E.2 The Firearms Operational Threshold
Firearms cases are at the apex of operational stakes. Procedural rigour intensified. Mandatory minimum
sentencing requires precise file-build.

E.3 The Custody Regime Discipline
Each detention regime has its specific framework. Authority at each stage. Time recording precise. Suspect
rights honoured. Member-in-Charge independence preserved.

E.4 The PULSE / PEMS Integrity Standard
The Garda information systems are evidentially significant. Accurate, contemporaneous, comprehensive
entries. Integrity is the case foundation.

E.5 The Disclosure Discipline
Disclosure is continuous. The investigation file is built with disclosure in mind. Sensitive material flagged
early; PII engaged where appropriate. The disclosure register is a file foundation.

E.6 The Command and Decision-Making Framework
The structured decision-making model and incident command framework support lawful, proportionate,
defensible action under operational pressure. Decision logs are the record.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Series

-

Volumes 1-9 together provide an operational reference framework for the Garda member - probationer or
experienced - across the full spectrum of investigation work in DMR North Central. The series is designed for
reference at the point of operational need, not for cover-to-cover reading.

Cross-Cutting Reference

(this volume)

How to Find What You Need
Substantive offence categories - Volumes 1-8 by topic.
Cross-cutting frameworks (powers, tools, structures) - Volume 9.
Case law for any topic - the Case Law Appendix of the relevant Volume + Volume 2 procedural foundations.
Statutory wording - the Statutory Anchors Appendix of the relevant Volume.
File craft for any offence - the Defences / Practice Appendix of the relevant Volume.
Specialist resource engagement - Volume 9 Chapter 5.

The Operational Disposition
These volumes can only be a reference. The Garda member's professional disposition - the curiosity, the
diligence, the respect for the people encountered, the rigorous attention to procedural integrity - that is what
makes the investigation work. The volumes support disposition; they do not substitute for it.

The Closing Reminder
Across the nine volumes, statutory framework and case law cited reflect the position at time of compilation. Irish
law, Garda operational policy, and ECHR / CJEU jurisprudence all continue to develop. Before relying on any
statement in these volumes for an operational decision, verify on revisedacts.lawreform.ie, irishstatutebook.ie,
vLex Ireland, BAILII, HUDOC (ECtHR), or curia.europa.eu (CJEU) as appropriate. Where Garda HQ directives
or operational policy apply, those take precedence over general guidance. Where doubt exists, supervisor
consultation is the operational standard.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Acknowledgements
The series draws on the developing body of Irish criminal law and policing scholarship; ECHR and CJEU
jurisprudence; Garda training material; the published case law; and the operational experience of An Garda
Siochana. Errors and omissions are inevitable in work of this scope; user feedback and ongoing revision
improve future editions.

INVESTIGATION

Volume 10 - Criminal Intelligence
Gathering, Building, Grading and Using Intelligence
DMR North Central - Mountjoy Garda Station

For probationer and experienced member reference

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 10 - A Note on the Material
Intelligence is the lifeblood of proactive policing. Almost every significant detection - the drugs seizure, the
firearm recovery, the disrupted robbery, the located fugitive - begins as a fragment of intelligence that someone
gathered, recorded, graded, and built upon. This volume sets out how that is done: lawfully, ethically,
systematically. It covers the old craft (beat knowledge, the collator, the cultivated source) and the new (digital
forensics, open-source, financial profiling, data analytics), and the framework that ties them together. Intelligence
work is also where the most serious risks to integrity, to sources' safety, and to citizens' rights arise - so the legal
and ethical discipline in this volume is not optional decoration. It is the work.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use Volume 10
Volume 10 is the intelligence volume. Where Volumes 1-8 cover what to do once an offence is identified, and
Volume 9 covers the cross-cutting tools, Volume 10 covers how intelligence is gathered, evaluated, built upon,
and turned into lawful action. Structure:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

What Intelligence Is - and Is Not
Intelligence is processed information that has been evaluated for reliability and given meaning through analysis,
to support a policing decision. The distinction between information, intelligence, and evidence is foundational:
Concept

Definition

Example

Information

Raw, unevaluated fact or report

'A man told me drugs are sold from No. 14.'

Intelligence

Information evaluated, graded, analysed,
given context

Graded report: reliable source, corroborated
by surveillance, linked to known dealer.

Evidence

Material admissible in court to prove a
fact

Drugs seized on warrant; surveillance log;
forensic certificate.

Intelligence guides action; evidence proves the case in court. Intelligence frequently cannot be used as evidence
(to protect a source, a method, or because it is hearsay). The skilled member understands this divide and works
within it - using intelligence to generate independent, evidentially-clean material (the 'sterile corridor', Chapter
8).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Old Ways and New Ways - The Continuum
Intelligence craft has a long lineage. The best practitioners blend the old and the new:
Old Ways

New Ways

Beat knowledge - who lives where, who associates
with whom

PULSE association mapping; analytical link charts

The Collator / card index

PULSE, GISS, intelligence
software

Cultivated community contacts and sources

Registered CHIS framework; structured source handling

Physical observation and foot surveillance

ANPR, CCTV networks, technical surveillance (2009
Act)

The pub conversation, the tip-off

OSINT - social media, marketplace, digital footprint

Following the money by reputation

Financial Intelligence Unit, STR analysis, CAB
profiling

Modus operandi recognition by experience

Crime-pattern analysis, hotspot mapping, series linkage

The briefing at parade

Tasking and Coordination meetings, intelligence
products

databases, analytical

Neither replaces the other. ANPR tells you a car moved; beat knowledge tells you why that matters. Social
media shows an association; a cultivated source explains its meaning. The new tools scale and document; the
old craft interprets and contextualises.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CYCLE - The Intelligence Cycle
C - Collect (from all source types).
Y - Yield to evaluation (grade source and information).
C - Collate (record, store, link).

SOURCE - Evaluating a Source
S - Standing - what is the source's access and history.
O - Origin - first-hand or hearsay.
U - Use history - has this source been reliable before.
R - Reason - what is the source's motivation.
C - Corroboration - what independently supports it.
E - Evaluate - assign the grade.

PLAN - Before Any Covert Activity
P - Power - what statutory authority (2009 Act, comms data, CHIS policy).
L - Lawful authorisation obtained at the correct level.
A - Articulated necessity and proportionality.
N - Necessity recorded - documented justification.

PROTECT - Source Handling
P - Policy - Garda CHIS framework applies.
R - Register - source formally registered.
O - Oversight - two-handler rule; controller; authorisation chain.
T - Tasking recorded.
E - Evaluate risk continuously - source welfare.
C - Corroborate before action.
T - 'Tell no one' - strict need-to-know; identity protected by PII.
Verification reminder
Intelligence law and policy continue to develop. The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 governs covert
surveillance; the Communications (Retention of Data) framework was reshaped by the Dwyer line and the 2022
Act; the Garda CHIS policy is an internal operational framework subject to revision; data protection in a lawenforcement context is governed by the Law Enforcement Directive as implemented in the Data Protection Act
2018. Verify current statutory text, case law, and Garda HQ policy before relying. Where covert powers are
engaged, supervisor and specialist (Crime and Security / specialist bureau) consultation is the operational
standard.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Criminal Intelligence Application
Intelligence work concentrates power and risk. Covert methods intrude on privacy; sources are placed in danger;
citizens are assessed and recorded on the basis of unproven reports. The Code of Ethics is therefore not a
constraint upon intelligence work but the condition of its legitimacy. Each Standard engages with particular
force.

Standard 1 - Duty
The duty to protect life shapes intelligence work directly. Intelligence located the threat-to-life; intelligence
enabled the interdiction; intelligence is the basis of the Osman warning to a person whose life is at risk. The
duty also extends to the source whose safety the member holds in trust.

Standard 2 - Honesty and Integrity
Intelligence integrity is the central discipline. Graded reports must reflect the genuine reliability of source and
information - inflating a grade to justify action is a serious integrity failure. Recording intelligence accurately,
neither exaggerating nor fabricating, is the foundation. A fabricated or embellished intelligence report can send
a warrant party to the wrong house, endanger a source, or convict an innocent person.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Intelligence-led operations frequently culminate in armed or forced intervention. The quality of the intelligence
shapes the proportionality of the response. Acting on poor, uncorroborated, or wrongly-graded intelligence can
lead to disproportionate or misdirected force.

Standard 5 - Respect and Equality
Intelligence must not be gathered, graded, or acted upon on the basis of improper factors - ethnicity, religion,
nationality, community, or association alone. Recording a person on an intelligence system carries lasting
consequences; it must rest on genuine policing grounds, not prejudice or profiling. The inner-city context, with
its diverse and sometimes over-policed communities, makes this especially live.

Standard 6 - Confidentiality
Need-to-know governs all intelligence. The handling code controls dissemination. Source identity is protected
absolutely within the lawful framework. Improper access to intelligence systems, gossip about sources, or
disclosure outside the framework are serious breaches that can cost lives.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Where intelligence is misused - fabricated, wrongly graded, accessed improperly, or used to target on improper
grounds - the duty to speak up applies. Where covert powers are exceeded, the duty applies. Intelligence abuses
are often invisible to outside scrutiny, which makes internal integrity and the willingness to speak up the
principal safeguard.

Seven Intelligence Trip-Wires

The Welfare Dimension - Sources and Members
Source handling carries a duty of care to the source, whose cooperation may place them at serious risk. It also
carries a psychological cost to the handler. Specialist source-handling structures, welfare frameworks, and
supervisory oversight exist for both reasons. Members engaged in sustained covert or source work should
engage with welfare support as a professional norm.

Compliments - Intelligence Categories

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

High-quality intelligence development leading to significant lawful detection.
Professional source handling - sustained, safe, productive, compliant.
Analytical work that links a series or unlocks an investigation.
Effective intelligence-led operation - proportionate, well-planned, clean.
Integrity in grading and recording - accurate even where less convenient.
Protecting a source or method under pressure within the lawful framework.
